subConcepts.name: Reading & Writing Numbers in Words and Digits

learningGoal: How to read and write numbers up to a million in words
Why we group digits in threes when writing large numbers

screens.title: How do you say 163,500?
screens.body: Ben reads the population sign for a town in Dorset. The number is **163,500**. Can you say it out loud? Big numbers look scary, but there's a simple trick to reading them!

screens.title: Split it into groups of three!
screens.body:
Big numbers are easier to read when you **split them into groups of three** from the right.

The left group is the **thousands**. The right group is the **ones**.

**163,500** = **one hundred and sixty-three thousand, five hundred**

steps.text: Split into groups from the right

steps.why: This tells you the place value

steps.result: one hundred and sixty-three thousand, five hundred

screens.title: Which one is correct?
screens.body: Which is the correct way to write **245,200** in words?

interaction.question: How do you write 245,200 in words?
options: two hundred and forty-five thousand, two hundred
two hundred and forty-five thousand and two hundred
two hundred and forty-five hundred, two hundred
twenty-four thousand, five hundred and two hundred
two hundred forty-five thousand, two hundred
interaction.correctAnswer: two hundred and forty-five thousand, two hundred
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **245,200** in words is: **two hundred and forty-five thousand, two hundred** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The correct way is: **two hundred and forty-five thousand, two hundred**. Remember to split the number into groups of three from the right.

screens.title: Reading big numbers — the recipe!
screens.body: Follow these steps for any large number:

steps.text: Step 1: Split into groups of three from the right
steps.why: Use commas to separate: thousands, millions, etc.

steps.text: Step 2: Read each group as a normal number
Step 3: Say the group name after each chunk

steps.why: 384,400 = three hundred and eighty-four thousand, four hundred ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid common mistakes when writing numbers in words
Why zeros matter as placeholders in large numbers

screens.title: Spot Holly's mistake!
screens.body: Holly writes a number from a newspaper article. The number is **205,300**, but Holly wrote it as: "twenty-five thousand, three hundred". Can you see what went wrong?

screens.title: Zeros are placeholders!
screens.body:
Holly missed the zero and read 205 as 25.

The **zeros** hold the other digits in their correct columns. Without them, the digits slide into the wrong places!

The correct reading is: **two hundred and five thousand, three hundred**

screens.title: What number did the words describe?
screens.body: Someone wrote "thirty-six thousand, two hundred". What number would that actually be in digits?

interaction.question: "thirty-six thousand, two hundred" would actually be which number?
interaction.correctFeedback: That's right! "thirty-six thousand, two hundred" = **36,200**, but the actual number is **306,200**. Zeros matter! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "thirty-six thousand, two hundred" = **36,200**. The actual number should be **306,200**.

steps.why: But the real number has a zero they missed!
three hundred and six thousand, two hundred

screens.title: Don't skip the zeros!
screens.body: Zeros are **placeholders** — they keep every digit in the right column. When reading or writing numbers:

steps.text: Check every column in the place value chart
A zero means 'nothing in this place'
steps.why: e.g. 205 has zero tens — it's not 25!

steps.text: Read each group of three carefully

subConcepts.name: Understanding the Value of Each Digit

learningGoal: How to find the value of any digit in a number
Why the position of a digit changes its value

screens.title: What is the 5 worth in 45,678?
screens.body: Ben reads the attendance at a football match. The total is **45,678**. The digit **5** appears in this number — but what is it actually **worth**? Is it just 5? Or something much bigger?

screens.title: The position tells you the value!

interaction.sentence: To find the value of a digit, multiply it by its ____ value
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Multiply the digit by its place value. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — you multiply the digit by its **place** value to find what it's worth!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: In the number **63,842**, what is the value of the digit **8**?

interaction.question: What is the value of the digit 8 in 63,842?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The 8 is in the hundreds column, so it's worth **800** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The 8 is in the **hundreds** column. 8 × 100 = **800**.

screens.title: Digit vs value — know the difference!
screens.body: The **digit** is the number you see. The **value** depends on its **position** in the number:

steps.text: Step 1: Find the digit in the number
steps.why: Which digit are you looking at?

steps.text: Step 2: Check which column it's in
Step 3: Multiply the digit by its place value

learningGoal: How to understand that the same digit has different values in different positions
Why position matters more than the digit itself

screens.title: Same digit, different value?
screens.body: Grace notices that the digit 3 appears twice in a number. Look at **33,456** — the digit **3** appears more than once! But are both 3s worth the same amount?

screens.title: How much more is it worth?
screens.body: In **66,821**, the **6** in the **ten-thousands** column is worth how many times more than the **6** in the **thousands** column?

interaction.question: How many times more is the ten-thousands 6 worth compared to the thousands 6?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! **60,000 ÷ 6,000 = 10**. Each column to the left is 10× bigger! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! **60,000 ÷ 6,000 = 10**. Each place to the left is 10 times bigger.

screens.title: Position changes everything!
screens.body: The same digit can be worth completely different amounts depending on where it sits:

steps.text: Each column is 10× bigger than the one to its right
steps.why: O → T → H → Th → TTh: ×10 each time

steps.text: The digit tells you HOW MANY of that place value
Same digit, different column = different value!

learningGoal: How to compare two numbers by checking digits from left to right
Why we start with the highest place value column

screens.title: Which is bigger: 34,567 or 34,589?
screens.body: Aisha compares the populations of two towns. One had **34,567** people and the other had **34,589**. Which number is larger? There's a step-by-step method!

interaction.sentence: When comparing numbers, start from the ____ and work right
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Always compare from the left — the highest place value decides first. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start from the **left** (the biggest place value column) and work right!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which symbol goes between these two numbers?

**57,312  ☐  57,348**

interaction.question: 57,312  ☐  57,348 — which symbol fits?
interaction.correctFeedback: That's right! **57,312 < 57,348**. The tens digit decided it: 1 vs 4 ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Look at the **tens** column: 1 vs 4. So **57,312 < 57,348**.

screens.title: Comparing numbers — the recipe!
screens.body: To compare any two numbers:

steps.text: Step 1: Check they have the same number of digits
steps.why: More digits = bigger number (unless leading zeros)
Start with the highest place value

steps.text: Step 3: Find the first column that's different
steps.why: That column decides which is bigger
Once you find a difference, you can stop ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid comparing numbers from the wrong end
Why starting from the right gives wrong answers

screens.title: Spot Charlie's mistake!
screens.body:
Charlie says: **45,892 < 45,867**

That doesn't look right! Can you see where Charlie went wrong?

screens.title: Which is correct?
screens.body:
What is the correct comparison?

**73,214  ☐  73,248**

interaction.question: Which is correct?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **73,214 < 73,248** — always compare from the left! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The tens column shows 1 vs 4. So **73,214 < 73,248**.

screens.title: Left to right — every time!
screens.body: The biggest mistake in comparing numbers is looking at the **wrong end**. Here's the recipe:

learningGoal: How to round to the nearest 1000, 10000 or 100000
How to find the decider digit for larger place values

screens.title: Round 156,732 to the nearest ten thousand
screens.body: Aisha estimates the number of visitors to a theme park. The exact number is **156,732**, but it needs rounding to the **nearest ten thousand**. The method is the same as rounding to the nearest 10 or 100 — just with bigger numbers!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Target → decider → apply the rule. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first find the target digit, then check the decider, then apply the rounding rule.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **283,415** rounded to the **nearest ten thousand**?

interaction.question: What is 283,415 rounded to the nearest ten thousand?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The decider is **3**, so we round **down**. **283,415 → 280,000** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The decider is **3**. 3 < 5 → round down. The answer is **280,000**.

screens.title: Rounding big numbers — same method!
screens.body: Whether you're rounding to the nearest 10 or 100,000, the method is always the same:

steps.why: The digit in the column you're rounding to

steps.text: Step 2: Look at the DECIDER (one place right)
steps.why: This digit decides up or down
The same rule, no matter how big the number

steps.text: Step 4: Replace everything to the right with zeros

learningGoal: How to avoid sequential (cascading) rounding
Why you should round in one step, not multiple steps

screens.title: Spot Charlie's mistake!
screens.body:
Charlie tries to round a number to the nearest thousand.

Charlie says: **4,449** rounded to the nearest thousand = **5,000**

That's not right! Can you see what went wrong?

screens.title: Round in ONE step — never cascade!
What's the correct answer?
screens.body: What is **4,449** correctly rounded to the **nearest thousand**?

interaction.question: 4,449 rounded to the nearest thousand = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: That's right! **4,449 → 4,000**. Round in one step, never cascade! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The decider is **4**, so we round **down**. The answer is **4,000**.

screens.title: Never cascade your rounding!
screens.body: A very common mistake is rounding in stages (10 → 100 → 1000). This gives the **wrong** answer!

learningGoal: How to round a decimal to the nearest whole number
How to round a decimal to one decimal place

screens.title: Round 1.47 to the nearest whole number
screens.body: Daisy measures the length of her desk in metres and gets **1.47 metres**. She needs to round it to the **nearest whole number**. The method is exactly the same as rounding whole numbers!

interaction.sentence: Rounding decimals uses the same ____ as rounding whole numbers
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The same method — find the target, check the decider, apply the 0-4/5-9 rule. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — rounding decimals uses the same **method** as whole numbers: target digit, decider digit, 0-4 down / 5-9 up!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **2.73** rounded to the **nearest whole number**?

interaction.question: What is 2.73 rounded to the nearest whole number?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The decider is **7**, so we round **up**. **2.73 → 3** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The decider is **7**. 7 ≥ 5 → round up. The answer is **3**.

screens.title: Rounding decimals — same recipe!
screens.body: Rounding decimals works **exactly** the same as rounding whole numbers:

steps.text: Nearest whole number → target is the ones digit
steps.why: Decider is the tenths (first decimal) digit

steps.text: One decimal place → target is the tenths digit
steps.why: Decider is the hundredths (second decimal) digit
Then drop everything after the target ✓

learningGoal: How to round money amounts to the nearest pound
When rounding decimals is useful in real life

screens.title: Quick estimate: how much altogether?
screens.body: Finn estimates the total cost of his shopping: bread £1.85, milk £1.15, apples £2.49. Instead of adding the exact amounts, you can **round each to the nearest pound** for a quick estimate (a rough guess)!

screens.title: Round to the nearest pound!
screens.body:
To round money to the nearest pound, look at the **pence** (the tenths digit).

• 50p or more → round **up**
• Less than 50p → round **down**

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Round **£7.62** to the nearest pound.

interaction.question: £7.62 rounded to the nearest pound = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Look at the pence. **£7.62 → £8**.

screens.title: Rounding money — a life skill!
screens.body: Rounding to the nearest pound is great for **quick estimates** when shopping:

steps.text: Look at the pence (digits after the point)
steps.why: 50p or more → round up. Less than 50p → round down

steps.text: Round each item to the nearest pound
Add the rounded amounts for a quick total
steps.why: It won't be exact, but it's close enough to check! ✓

learningGoal: How to partition a number into thousands, hundreds, tens and ones
Why partitioning helps with mental addition and subtraction

screens.title: Break 4,567 into pieces!
screens.body:
Ella breaks down a number for a mental maths challenge. The number is **4,567**.

**Partitioning** means splitting a number into its place value parts. It makes adding and subtracting in your head much easier — instead of working with one big number, you deal with smaller, simpler pieces!

screens.title: Each digit becomes its full value!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Write values, skip zeros, then check by adding. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first write each digit's full value, then skip zeros, then add up to check.

screens.title: What's the full partition?
screens.body: What does **7,321** partition into? Add up all the parts!

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Each digit keeps its column value: **7,000 + 300 + 20 + 1**.

screens.title: Partitioning — a powerful tool!
screens.body: Partitioning means breaking a number into its **place value parts**:

steps.text: Write each digit with its full place value
Check: the parts should add back to the original!

learningGoal: How to use partitioning for mental addition
When to use partitioning in real calculations

screens.title: 346 + 235 in your head!
screens.body: Ben adds two numbers mentally using partitioning: **346 + 235**. That looks tricky to do mentally — but **partitioning** makes it easy!

screens.title: Partition, add, combine!
screens.body: Break both numbers into hundreds, tens and ones. Then add each group separately:

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Use partitioning to work out **346 + 235**.

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 346 + 235 = **581**. Partitioning makes mental maths so much easier! ✓

screens.title: Partition to add mentally!
screens.body: Partitioning turns one hard addition into three easy ones:

steps.text: Add the hundreds, then tens, then ones
steps.why: Each step is a simple calculation

learningGoal: How to see that adding 10, 100, or 1000 changes only one digit
Why the other digits stay the same

screens.body: Daisy watches numbers change on a scoreboard. The number goes from **4,567** up by **100**. Which digit changes? Let's find out!

interaction.correctFeedback: That's right! Only the hundreds digit changes: 5 → 6. **4,667** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Adding 100 only changes the hundreds digit. **4,567 + 100 = 4,667**.

screens.title: The magic of powers of 10!
screens.body: Adding 10, 100, or 1000 is super easy once you know the trick:

steps.text: Adding 10 → only the TENS digit goes up by 1
Adding 100 → only the HUNDREDS digit goes up by 1
Adding 1,000 → only the THOUSANDS digit goes up by 1
Subtracting works the same way — the digit goes DOWN by 1

learningGoal: How to avoid changing the wrong digit when adding 10, 100 or 1000
Why carrying sometimes changes more than one digit

screens.title: Spot Charlie's mistake!
screens.body:
Charlie subtracts 100 from a number.

Charlie says: **5,023 − 100 = 5,123**

That's not right! What went wrong?

interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Remember to change the thousands digit. **7,841 − 1,000 = 6,841**.

screens.title: Watch out for carries!
screens.body: Usually only one digit changes, but sometimes it **carries** into a new column:

learningGoal: How to arrange digits to make the largest possible number
How to arrange digits to make the smallest possible number

screens.title: What's the biggest number you can make?
screens.body: Jake has these digit cards: **3, 7, 1, 9, 5**. Using each digit exactly once, what's the **largest** number you can make? And the **smallest**?

screens.title: What's the largest?
screens.body: Using the digits **3, 7, 1, 9, 5**, what is the **largest** number you can make?

interaction.question: Largest number from digits 3, 7, 1, 9, 5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Put the biggest digits first: **97,531** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Sort the digits from biggest to smallest: 9, 7, 5, 3, 1 → **97,531**.

steps.why: Put the biggest digit in the highest place

screens.title: The sorting shortcut!
screens.body: Making the largest or smallest number from digits is easy:

steps.why: Biggest digit in the highest place value
Smallest digit in the highest place value
Zero can't be the first digit — swap it with the next smallest ✓

learningGoal: How to handle zero when making the smallest number
Why the first digit can never be zero in a whole number

screens.title: The zero trap!
screens.body:
Holly tries to make the smallest number from these digits: **5, 0, 3, 8, 0**. She sorts them smallest first: **0, 0, 3, 5, 8** and writes **00358**.

But wait — that starts with zero! Is it really a 5-digit number?

steps.why: Starting with 0 gives a 3-digit number (358), not a 5-digit number

screens.title: Zero can't be first!
screens.body:
A number can't start with zero — **00358** is really just **358**.

Instead, put the **smallest non-zero digit** (3) first, then put the zeros next:

steps.why: Swap 0 with the next smallest non-zero digit

steps.text: Put 3 first, then zeros, then the rest

screens.title: What's the correct smallest number?
screens.body: Using the digits **5, 0, 3, 8, 0**, what is the correct **smallest** 5-digit number?

interaction.question: Smallest 5-digit number from 5, 0, 3, 8, 0?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Put 3 first, then the zeros: **30,058** ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The smallest non-zero digit (3) goes first, then the zeros. Answer: **30,058**.

screens.title: The zero rule!
screens.body: When making the smallest number from digits that include zero:

steps.text: Sort all digits from smallest to biggest
If the smallest is 0, swap it with the next non-zero digit
The zeros go right after the first digit
